title: Knot Theory and Machine Learning
speaker: Andras Juhasz
abstract: The signature of a knot K in the 3-sphere is a classical  invariant that gives a lower bound on the genera of compact oriented surfaces in the 4-ball with boundary K. We say that K is hyperbolic if its complement admits a complete, finite volume hyperbolic metric. I will explain how we have used methods from machine learning to find an unexpected relationship between the signature and the cusp shape of a hyperbolic knot. This is joint work with Alex Davies, Marc Lackenby, and Nenad Tomasev.

proof_stages.label: Signature and hyperbolic cusp invariants
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the two kinds of knot invariants to be related: the signature from the Seifert form as a four-dimensional invariant, and hyperbolic knot invariants such as volume and maximal-cusp longitude and meridian translations.

proof_stages.label: Supervised-learning search for a signature signal
proof_stages.summary: Datasets of knots are assembled with hyperbolic feature vectors and signature labels, a feed-forward neural-network classifier is trained and tested, and its accuracy well above baseline is interpreted as evidence for a hidden relationship.

proof_stages.label: Saliency discovery of the natural slope
proof_stages.summary: Feature-attribution methods identify cusp translations as dominant, and plotting them leads to the natural slope Re(lambda/mu), its geometric winding interpretation, and an observed near-linear relation with twice the signature.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the first volume-error conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The data-motivated conjecture that twice the signature minus the natural slope is controlled linearly by hyperbolic volume is shown to be false using highly twisted knots whose odd-linking twists make slope and signature have different asymptotics while volume stays bounded.

proof_stages.label: Injectivity-radius corrected bound
proof_stages.summary: After defining the injectivity radius outside the maximal cusp, the corrected theorem bounds the difference between twice the signature and the natural slope by a constant times volume divided by the cube of the injectivity radius.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for surgery and four-ball genus
proof_stages.summary: The corrected bound is illustrated numerically and then converted into consequences for hyperbolic Dehn surgery and into lower bounds on the topological four-ball genus using hyperbolic cusp data.

proof_stages.label: Short-geodesic correction theorem
proof_stages.summary: To remove the injectivity-radius denominator, the talk introduces odd short geodesics, torus-knot signature defects, and twisting parameters, then states a refined theorem bounding the corrected signature-slope difference linearly by volume.

examples.name: Highly twisted odd-linking knots
examples.note: Twisting along circles linking an odd number of strands, especially the three-strand case, is presented as the counterexample that breaks the first volume-error conjecture and points toward odd short-geodesic correction terms.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the twisting construction, derives asymptotics for slope and signature, and computes the three-strand case giving -9 versus -8 times the twist

passes.reason: The large-twist odd-linking family is an asymptotic thin/extreme regime and is introduced precisely to violate the initial volume-error conjecture.
